title: Slab rectangular trinket tray
subtitle: A small flat tray with folded-up rim for keys and coins
excerpt: A small rectangular tray, 14 cm by 9 cm, with the four edges folded upward and the corners reinforced from inside with short clay coils. No kiln required; air-dry clay holds the format well at this size. Decorative use for keys, coins, jewellery, or small dried botanicals.
sourceNotes: Folded-rim slab construction derived from first principles. V&A open-access collection for trinket-tray form reference.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness.
Scratching joining surfaces and brushing them with slurry before pressing together to bond two clay pieces.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim cleanly.
Air-dried but unfired. For air-dry clay, the finished structural state.

content.text: A small rectangular tray, 14 cm by 9 cm with a 20 mm folded-up rim, sized for the keys-and-coins corner of a hallway shelf. The whole tray is one
slab
with the four edges folded upward and the corners reinforced from inside with short coils. No kiln required; air-dry clay handles this format well at the small scale.
Preparation
Wedge the clay for two or three minutes on the board to even the moisture and clear air pockets. Reserve 20 g for the corner reinforcing coils. The rest forms the slab.
Building the tray
1. Roll the slab

Pat the clay into a thick rectangle and lay the guide rails on either side. Roll between the rails for an even 6 mm thickness. The rolled slab should measure about 20 cm by 15 cm; this allows for a 20 mm fold on each side around a 14 cm by 9 cm base.
2. Cut to size
Cut a 18 cm by 13 cm rectangle from the centre of the slab with the fettling knife held straight up. The trimmings go back into the clay bag for re-wedging.
3. Score the fold lines
Lightly score a line 20 mm in from each edge using the needle tool against a straight ruler. These four lines mark where the sides fold up. Score shallow; the fold needs to bend, not cut through.
4. Fold the sides

Working on one long side at a time, slide a thin ruler or fingertips under the edge and fold the strip upward along the scored line. The side stands at about 90 degrees. Support the base with the other hand underneath while folding. Do the opposite long side next, then the two short ends.
5. Reinforce the corners
Each corner now has a small triangular gap where the two folded sides meet.
Score and slip

the inside faces of each corner. Roll four short coils about 4 mm thick and 25 mm long from the reserved clay. Press one coil into each interior corner with a finger, blending it into both walls. Smooth flush with the rib.
6. Refine the rim

Run the wooden rib along the top edge of each side to compress and smooth. Any uneven height at the corners can be trimmed level with the fettling knife held flat. Sponge once around the top to round the edge.
Drying
The sides may lean outward while still soft. Brace them with small blocks of clay or jar lids against the outside faces until the tray reaches
leather-hard
. Cover loosely with plastic for the first 12 hours. Remove the braces and the plastic together once the walls hold upright independently. Full drying to
bone-dry
takes one to two days.
Finishing

Once bone-dry, sand the rim, the corners, and the underside with 220-grit sandpaper. Brush off the dust. Seal inside and out with two coats of PVA or acrylic varnish. Paint with acrylics before sealing if a colour finish is wanted. Self-adhesive felt pads on the four underside corners protect any wood surface the tray sits on.
Care
Air-dry clay is not waterproof or food-safe. Use the tray for keys, coins, jewellery, paperclips, or small dried botanicals. Wipe with a damp cloth; do not submerge or wash under a running tap.
Variations
Press a leaf or lace texture into the slab before cutting for a patterned base. The texture reads through to the inside of the tray once the sides are folded up.
Cut a square version (10 cm by 10 cm) for a desk paperclip dish. Same method, smaller cut.
Pinch a small spout into one short end while still soft, for a tray that doubles as a small jewellery scoop.

items.symptom: Corners crack apart as the tray dries
items.cause: Corner gaps not reinforced with coils
items.fix: Press a small coil into each inside corner. Blend it into both walls. If a crack has already formed, press paper clay slurry into it before sealing

items.symptom: Sides lean outward and the rim flares
items.cause: Clay was too soft when folded
items.fix: Brace the outside walls with small blocks until the clay reaches leather-hard; let the slab rest 5 minutes after rolling before folding

items.symptom: Base warps or curves up at the corners
items.cause: Base dried faster than the folded sides
items.fix: Cover with plastic for the first 12 hours; press the tray gently flat onto the board while still leather-hard to correct any rise
